held at Edinburgh, in the Hall of the Antiquarian Society, on

the 21st November 1853:
RESOLVED, ~

That the Registrum Reeentius Abbacte Ve Aberbrothok, (1325-
15236,) along with an Abbreviation of Charters contained in the later
Registers of the Abbey, and Copies of Original Charters at Panmure,
or from other sources, be printed for the Members of the Cues, umi-
formly with the former Volume, and under the joint superintendence of

Cosmo Innes, Esq., and Parrick Cuanwens, of Auldbar, Msq.: and.

further, that a limited impression upon dillerent paper, not to exceed
One Hundred Copies, be provided for the Subseribers upon the same
terms as had previously been arranged with the Committee of the Cie.

for the Registrum Vetus.
Extracted from the Minutes,

DAVID LAING, Secrerary.

PREFACE.

Tue First Volume of this Chartulary ends with the reign of
Robert I. It contains the charters and documents recorded in
the Older Register of the Abbey and those coming within its
period, though found elsewhere recorded. The present volunie,
commencing in 1329—the first year of King David L1.—includes
the register known as the Registrum Nigrum, Registrum Recen-
tius, or Black Book, and the contents of the various later volumes
of Register described in the Preface to the First Volume.

The early Abbots are enumerated in the Preface to the First
Volume.

17. Geoffry, the seventeenth known Abbot of Arbroath, held
the Abbacy from 1328 at least till the end of 1842. (30th
December.) He was of the party that submitted at first to
Edward Balliol, in 1382.

18. William was Abbot on 17th July 1848, when he vindi-
cated the Abbey’s privilege of freedom from toll against the
Burgh of Dundee, in the Court of the Justiciar at Forfar,' and
he was still in the Abbacy on 18th July 1366.

19. We find John Abbot in 1370; and we are well pleased
to learn the surname of this Abbot, which is associated with the
first formation of a harbour at Arbroath. His name was John
Gedy ; and John was still Abbot on 3d Inne 1895.2 His seal is
found appended to the Act of Parliament settling the suecession
to the Crown, 1871.

20, Walter Paniter, of the family of Newmanswalls, seems to
have been the next Abbot. Walter is found Abbot on 11th
December 1411.4 The Abbot is named Walter Paniter in
November 1417, and again in September 1433.° Being old, he

igned in favour of

21. Richard Guthrie, the Prior, who was Abbot at least as carly
as 2d October 1450,° and who in his turn resigned on the 18th
December 1455.’ Ifis successor recorded of him, that he was
“ noeeht active nor gaif intendens for remeid of vrangis dwne to
the haly place.”*

22. Maleohn Brydy, previously Prior of the subordinate House
of Fyvie, was Abbot of Arbroath on the 27th July 1456, and
held the office till 1470," when he is found in high contention
with Patrick, Bishop of St. Andrews, whom he accused of ex-

PREFACE. xi

tortion and oppression, especially in visiting the monastery, not
in pastoral manner and with lawful number of followers, but
with a hundred or two hundred horsemen in company. The
Bishop had proceeded to extremities, and thrown poor Abbot
Malcolm into his dungeon at St. Andrews, (aretis carceribus,) and
he is next mentioned as deprived of his benefice,’ 3d November
1470, when the monks proceeded to elect an abbot in lis room,
and their choice fell upon another

28. Richard Guthrie, the Prior of the Convent, who granted a
charter as Abbot, on the 20th May 1471," but died or demitted
soon after, for we find

24. George Abbot of Arbroath on the 29th July 1472 ;° who
continued Abbot till his death in 1482.*

25, On the 8th day of August 1482, the Convent unanimously
eleeted as Abbot, William Bonkil, a monk of the Abbey. We
have the speech of the sub-Prior in proposing him. He told the
monks he was “a man come of good family, meek, quict, and
zealous for peace, loving God and the Church ; humble, pious,
sweet-tempered, and of good manners ; a great counsellor and
defender of the Chureh in its affairs, likewise charitable and
good, of age about fifty, a bountiful almsgiver, very discreet in
spiritual and temporal matters, born of lawful wedlock, affable,
a good friend, and mereiful in the communion of the faithful.”®
Abbot William Bonkil died in the year 1483.

26. The Convent being assembled for the election of his sue-

cessor, (29th July 14838,) and being divided in opinion, in the
presence of William Archbishop of St. Andrews, by his advice,
and by way of compromise, both parties of the Monks elected
Sir Alexander Masoun Prior of Fyvie, “ compromissar,’ for
choosing the Abbot. The Compromissar forthwith postulated
Sir David Lichtone, clerk of the King’s treasury and arch-
deacon of Ross, to be Abbot, who was received “ with great
joy ;” and 3000 dueats of gold voted for the expense of expedit-
ing his bulls at Rome ;? a sum which long burdened the Abbey.
David Lichtone was still Abbot on 17th December 1502.

27. After his death, James, Duke of Ross, Archbishop of St.
Andrews, brother to King James IV., held the Abbacy in com-
mendam during the short period which he survived.

28. On the 8d day of February 1508, the Chapter met to elect
an Abbot in his room, and chose unanimously George Hepburn,
of the family of Bothwell, Provost of Linclouden,” who ruled
the Mona

--- Aberbroth_page29.png ---
PREFACE. Mili

then beginning his career of power, He was Abbot of Arbroath
on 18th January 1524.’ The last of his charters as Abbot of
Arbroath that is recorded, and the last of this Register, is
dated 1536.’ Te was made Cardinal in 1538; succeeded his
uncle as Archbishop of St. Andrews in 1539; was appointed
Chancellor of Scotland in 1543; Legate va latere in 1544; and
was murdered in his Castle of St. Andrews 29th May 1546. He
did not, however, hold the Abbacy of Arbroath to his death.

Arbroath, like other Monasteries, had fallen in trouble. The
election, by the Convent, which had perhaps for some time been
nominal, had now quite disappeared, and the rich benetive be-
came the prey of each successive faction in power.

In 1541, in the regency of the Earl of Arrane, and during
the short disgrace of the Cardinal Archbishop, the Abbacy of
Arbroath was given 7a commendum to Johu Ilamilton, the
Regent’s second son, afterwards first Marquis of Hamilton. His

possession was not undisputed : George Douglas, a natural son

of Archibald, Earl of Angus, and subsequently Bishop of Mor
appears to have had an ineffectual grant of the Abbacy, after
the death of the Cardinal ; and James Betoun was also styled
Postulate of Aberbrothok and Abbot, from 1548 down to the
period of his elevation to the See of Glasgow in 1551. On the
attainder of the Hamiltons in 1579, the Abbacy of Arbroath was
again regarded as vacant ; and the King bestowed it cx commen-
dam upon his cousin, Esme Stuart of Aubigny, whom he created

No, 583, = Nv. 839.


--- Aberbroth_page30.png ---
xiv PREFACE.

Duke of Lennox, and who seems to have held the emoluments
of the Abbacy till his death in 1583.

The Haimiltons were soon after restored, and on the llth
November 1600, James Hamilton (afterwards second Marquis of
Hamilton) had a charter under the Great Seal of the Abbaey of
Aberbrothwiek ; and on the 6th July 1606, the King, with con-
sent of Parliament, dissolved from the Crown the lands, patron-
ages, and teinds of the Abbey, (“being in his Majesteis handis
be dimissionn and resignatioun maid thairof be the Abbot and
Convent of the samin,”) and‘ erected the same into a temporal
lordship in his favour, with the dignity and title of a Lord of
Parliament.

We are informed by John Spotiswood? that the Abbaecy of
Arbroath “ afterwards belonged to the Earl of Dysart, from
* whom Patrick Manle of Panmure, gentleman of the bedcham-
“ ber to King James VI., did purchase it, with the right of
** patronage of thirty-four parish churches belonging thereto.”

The contents of the present volume are very different in
charaeter from those of the former, which derived its chief
interest from the records of the numerous endowments of the
Abbey during the reigns of William the Lion, its founder, and
his son and grandson. The age of church endowments was
gone, and the Abbey Registers, after the reign of Bruce, show

"Acts of Parlianent 2 Account of the Houses of Scotland.



--- Aberbroth_page31.png ---
PREFACE. XV

no accession of property or wealth, and record only the ad-
ministration of the lands, churches and tithes of the great
Monastery, which brought it however into contact with all the
lords of Angus and Mearns, and with those of several districts
of other counties.

It is not in Protestant Britain alone that it requires some re-
flection to appreciate fully the station filled of old by the inmates
of our greater monasteries. In the Roman Catholic countries of
modern Europe, it is hardly less difficult to realize the days when
the clergy, secular and regular, engrossed all the learning and

accomplishment, and a large share of the wealth, luxury, know-
ledge of the world, and social influence of the community. It is
to be remarked, that in Scotland, as in other countries, while
the secular or parochial clergy were often the younger sons of
good families, the convents of monks and friars were recruited
wholly from the lower classes ; and yet,—not to speak of the daily
bread, the freedom from daily care, all the vulgar temptations of
such a life in hard times—the life of a monk opened no mean
path to the ambitious spirit. The offices of the monastery alone
might well seem prizes, to be contended for by the son of the

peasant or burgess, and the highest of these placed its holder on

a level with the greatest of the nobility.

The Lord Abbot of such a house as Arbroath, whether bearing
crosier and mitre, or buckling on more carnal armour, whether
sitting in the high places of Council and Parliament, or taking

homage and dispensing law among his vassals and serfs, or fol-


--- Aberbroth_page32.png ---
xvi PREFACE.

lowing his sovereign to battle, was, in virtue of his social posi-
tion, his revennes, his followers, and actnal power, by far the
greatest personage of the shire.

The abbey was toll frec, that is, protected against the local
impositions which of old beset all merchandise ; and the Abbot
vindicated the freedom of his “men” against the exactions of the
Bailies of Dundee, who had presumed to levy a penny from his
stallinger in the fair of their Burgh.’ Tt was custom free, and

passed its exports of wool, hides, tallow, salmon, by virtue of its

own coket.? But the privilege the Abbot most valued, (and in-
trinsically the most valnable,) was the tenure of all his lands ‘in
free regality, that is, with sovereign power over his people, and
the unlimited emoluments of criminal jurisdiction? In 1435,
the Abbot, in virtue of that right of regality, componnded with
Andrew of Lyehtonn, and granted him a remission for the
slaughter of James Gibsoun.’ Long afterwards—after the Re-
formation had passed over abbot and monk, the lord of regality
had still the same power, and the Commendator of Arbroath was
able to rescue from the King’s Justiciar, and to “ repledge” into
his own court four men acensed of the slaughter of William Sib-
bald of Cair—as dwelling within his bounds (quasi infra bondas
gjusdem eommorantes.)’

The officer who administered this formidable jurisdiction.
was the Bailie of the Regality, as he was usually styled, or

5 Pite:

minal Trials, 1570, p. 16,



--- Aberbroth_page33.png ---
PREFACE. xvii

“Justiciar Chamberlain and Bailic,” as his style ran when in
1485 Abbot Lichtoun conferred the survivorship of the office
on two Ogilvies.’. At that period, whatever may have been
the case at the time of the battle of Brechin, the Bailiary
had become virtually hereditary in the family of Airlie.

The Mair and Coroner of the Abbey, (the ‘ Dereth” was per-
haps the same office in Celtie speech,) were the exeeutors of the
law within the bounds of the regality.’ Each office had lands
attached to it, affording part of the emolument of the officer.
The oftice of Judex, Deemster or Dempster in the Abbot's
Court was in like manner attached to a portion of the lands of
Caraldston, (deriving their name from that Keraldus who first
held the office,) and passed with it through the hands of the
Earl of Crawford and later owners, down to the abolition of
heritable jurisdictions.

The best of the shire and of neighbouring districts, thought it

no degradation to hold their lands as vassals of the great Abbey.

Record was made of the homage done by those barons to the
Justiciar of the Regality,—kneeling on the ground with hands
joined.’ For the most part they gave suit and service in the
Abbot's Court, and such other serviees as vassals of old really
performed to their superiors. Many were bound to give agri-
cultural service, harvest labour, and carriage of corn, wool,
wood, peats, and slates. But military service exempted from
preedial service ; and when a vassal was bound to follow the

1 No, 281. 2 No. 146, 148, 318. 3337.


--- Aberbroth_page34.png ---
xviii PREFACE.

Abbot to war, either with the northern lords, under the Breeben-
nach—the Banner of Colhunba—or under the proper standard of
the Abbey itself, he was free from the common duties owed to
the superior.!

We have little information of the early history of the Burgh
of Arbroath. With such protection for shipping as its natural
harbour afforded. it had grown up under the shelter and protec-
ion of the great Monastery, from a fishing hamlet, till it became
a place of some forcign trade in the fonrteenth century. The
worthy Abbot, John Gedy, saw the advantage that would arise
to lis town and the whole district, if, on that inhospitable coast,
ie could transform the creek among treacherous rocks into a

tolerably safe harbour; and the covenant made between the

Abbot and the burgesses for that object, on the 2d of April

304, as it is the oldest, is also perhaps the most curious and
interesting of the records of harbour-making and also of volun-

tary taxation in Scotland?

PREFACE. xix

The Abbey church and conventual buildings, begun in 1178 ;
sufficiently advanced in 121£ to be the burial-place of their
royal founder; and probably completed in 1233, when the
church was dedicated ;! were not allowed to decay through age,
and the gradual operation of time and the elements. They
suffered more than once by fire. Fordun relates, that in the
deadly year 1272, (when the land was barren, the sea unproduc-
tive, the air stormy, and when there was sickness among men,
and mortality of eattle,) ou Saturday of the octaves of the Epi-
phany, about midnight, a violent wind from the north coming
on suddenly with hail, blew down houses, smothered those sleep-
ing within, and tumbled down lofty buildings ; and that fire
breaking ont in consequence, burnt the chureh of Arbroath,

and many othe Boece, as usual, adds some cireumstances.

Not only were churehes and houses everywhere thrown down,
but the church towers were burnt, and the bells (que preciosis-
sima materia confieiuntur) partly broken, partly melted. Among
which the most remarkable were those whieh hung in the
towers of the chureh of Arbroath, which church was consumed
along with them.®

A century later, in 1330, the chronicler informs us that the
monastery of Arbroath was again accidentally burnt! It was

safely take the harbour, and there have safe berth,
nothwithstanding the ebb and flow of the sea, If it
shonld bappen, as God forbid, that the harbour in pro:

“ tery of Arbroath, placed on the brink of the sea, had suffered

“ English shipping.”!

We may readily conceive, withont proof of record, that the
Abbey buildings suffered no less damage in affrays of their
landward neighbours. Such for instanee was that “ discord
“ qnhilk fell betweine [the Lindesays and the Ogilvies] for anc
“ meane bailiarie of Arebroath, quhilk pertenit to Alexander
“ Lindsay ; bot Alexander Ogilvie, quhidder it cam of his awin
“ ambitione or if it was the Abbottis pleasour it is not certain

“ the same.”? The “ discord” was in the winter of 1445, and is
thus summarily noticed by a contemporary who cared for neither
faction :—

Tt is said
the Abbey church was again burned on this occasion, and not
improbably. We know not if the Abbey escaped an attack
planned inst it a century later, on 17th April 15H. The
Lords of the English Council reported to King Henry VIIL., that
Wyshart, among other enterpris

, undertook that a body of
troops to be paid by the English king, “joining with the power
~ of the Earl Marshall. the Master of Rothes, the laird of Calder,
‘and others of the Lord Gray's friends, will take upon them
“2... to destroy the Abbey and Town of Arbroth, being the
» Cardinal’s, and all the other Bishops and Abbots housex, and

countries on that side the water thereabouts.” Henry, who
was very wroth against the Cardinal, gave them all encourage-
ment, “effectually to burn and destroy.”*

Notwithstanding those partial conflagrations, and all the in-
juries of foreign enemies and rough neighbours, the Abbey of
Arbroath maintained its pre-eminence as among the first if not

the greatest of Scotch religious houses, from its earliest period

down to the Reformation. “ Erant autem,” says a historian of

the ith century, “due in Scotia famosissime abbatie, in
* facultatibus opulentissime et in wdificiis munitissime, Abre-
* bredoc ect Domfermelin.”* It was in the spacious buildings of

Anchinle

* Hamilton Papers, Maitland Miscell , vol.iv. p. 96


--- Aberbroth_page39.png ---
PREFACE. XNiii

this great Monastery that Robert Bruce, in April 1320. assembled
the Parliament which asscried in such vigorous language, in
their letter to the Pope, the freedom of their country. In 1470.
we find anew dormitoréun building, with timber brought from
Norway ;! and in the year 1488, it is incidentally noticed, the

Abbey entertained the King and his suite twice, the Archbishop

thrice, besides y

of the Lords of the Realm, and other hospi-
tality kept2

The scattered fragments of the Monastic buildings which sull
remain, disguised and injured as they have been by injudicious
repairs, furnish specimens of nearly every style of architecture
from the era of the dedication of the Abbey down to the century
which preceded the Reformation. They are now kept in decent
condition, and protected from further dilapidation.

of his successor and a vast assemblage of the nobles of Scotland.

PREFACE.

XXIV

chancel in front of the high altar place, was at once judged to
be that of the great founder. The coftin, of stone, was found to
contain only a portion of the bones of a man of good stature,
not much decayed. Its cover, of a blue shelly marble, had
upon it the mutilated sculpture which is figured at the end of
the first volume.’ It was hoped that the head and other frag-
ments of the monument might be found, but none have as yet
been discovered.

At the same time was discovered (in one of the western

towers,) a mutilated figure of a crosiered eeclesiastic, of the fine

sandstone of the district. It is one of those effigies which stood
under the niches that ornament all our older churches, and is
of good workmanship for that purpose. The lace, especially, is
very elaborate and sharply wrouglit, and when first discovered
still preserved some remains of the gold Ieaf with which it had
been ornamented. It is commonly said to be the statue of St.
Thomas A’Becket, the Patron, but on no better grounds than
the reeumbent monument is ascribed to the Founder of the
Abbey.

--- Aberbroth_page41.png ---
PREFACE. XXV

The Registers of Arbroath are not so rich as some others in
subjects of interest to the general antiquary. A few sueh how-
ever are suggested or illustrated by the present Volume.

The Abbot, on aeeount of the perils of crossing the sea to St.
Andrews, obtained from Rome the privilege of conferring minor
orders, and conseerating the furniture of the altar.'

The much-vexed claim of Subsidy was virtually enforced against
the Monastery by both Dioecsans—the Bishops of St. Andrews
and Brechin, though resisted, and paid under protest.?

Herrings in salt and in barrel are paid as rent from Inverness,
as if they were not then found off the Arbroath coast.’

For a permission to take bait from the shores of Monytieth,
the white fishers of the north ferry of Portincrag (Broughty), paid
for every day’s fishing of each small line six white fish! Several
documents show the jealousy with which the rights of sea fishing
were protected, at a time when it is commonly supposed the pro-
duce of the sea was not yet appropriated.’

Notices are found of early banking, and something resembling
foreign bills of exchange.’

T have met with only one notice of books, which were volumes
of Canon Law, evidently of much mercantile value.’

1 No, 5A. and Luvel not to receive the Abbot

--- Aberbroth_page42.png ---
XXvi PREFACE.

The only recorded covenant with a schoohnaster for instrnet-
ing the novices and young brethren, is unfortunately silent as to
the branches of learning they were to be taught. Mr. Archibald
Lamy, the pedagogue, has 10 marks of salary—the customary
and almost legal stipend of a parochial viear—besides his daily
portion with the monks."

The ‘Advoeate’ of the Abbey, receiving a yearly pension of

20 marks for his counsel and ‘advoeation,’ was no less a person-
age than Master James Henrison, the Clerk of Justiciary, or, as
we write the office, Lord Justice-Clerk.

The supplication and complaint of Abbot Malcolm to Parlia-
ment and Convocation, must have been drawn by a less prac-
tised hand. It is a very curious specimen of untechnical legal
pleading as well as of idiomatic Scotch language.”

We owe to David Betoun, on his first coming into the Abbacy,
some rues for its economy, which show the yearly consumption
of all supplies by the Convent. The monks used annually 800
wedders, and 9 score of marts, besides lamb and veal, swine,
erice, and chickens ; eggs and butter; dried fish (keeling, had-
dock and spelding), large supplies of fresh sea fish, and 11
barrels of salmon, the produce of their fishings at Dundee, the
Ferry (Broughty), and Montrose. The allowance of wheat was
30 chalders, of oatmeal 40 chalders, and of malt 82 chalders.
The officers are rebuked for negligence in letting the Convent

fixed by the Abbot's name between 1456 and
The guerela, though withont date, is 1470.

But the real and paramount interest of a record like the pre-
sent, is in furnishing directly or collaterally what may be con-
sidered the territorial history of the province. There is hardly
a barony in Angus and Mearns which docs not receive illustra-
tion from the records of Arbroath ; scarcely a funily of note
which must not seek its carly history among the transactions of
the great Abbey. The former volume is said to have dis-
appointed the gentlemen of Angus, who expected to have found
their ancestors there chronicled. They had not considered how
many of our ancient families went down in the War of Inde-
pendence,—how few of our present aristocracy trace back be-
yond the revolution of families and property which took place
under Bruce. The great old Earls of Angns, Fife, and Strath-

ern, are little more than mythological personages to the modern

genealogist. The De Berkeleys, De Valoins, De Malherbe

Mauleverers, De Monte-alto, De Monteforts, have not even left
their high sounding names in the country they once ruled.
Durward and Cumming, as great as any of them, have fallen

. It will be observed, the Cellarer’s de- poultry, spices, &c,, leaving to the (ira
‘charges,’ embrace only the

peat, fish, and malt.


--- Aberbroth_page44.png ---
xxviii PREFACE.

into humble life. It is the common case all over Scotland. It
is more surprising that some families of the Ante-Brusian mag-
nates of Angus still flourish. Lindsay and Ramsay, Ogilvy and
Maule, are no ignoble representatives of the old seignory. No
such disappointment, however, can reasonably be felt with regard
to the present volume. A large proportion of the extant families
of the two shires will find their ancestry illustrated in it; and,
though Carnegies and Guthries, Burnetts and Irvines, and a few
others, need no such help, it may yet come to pass that it will be
held a proof of gentry in Angus and Mearns, to be able to point
to an ancestor in the Chartulary of Arbroath.

The materials of this part of the Abbey records have been con-
siderably abridged. The charters contained in the Black Book.
and the first two volumes of the Regality Registers, coming down
to the year 1513, are given in the precise terms of the ori-
ginal, only discarding the words not required for the sense or
interest of the writ, and indicating by points such omissions.
The last volume, containing the Registers of the two Betons—
1513-36—is still more condensed, each charter being merely de-
scribed, still however in the language, and as nearly as prac-
ticable in the terms, of the original.

At the end of the Registers are added a few ancient charters,
printed, with only two exceptions, from originals at Panmure, for
the use of which the Club is indebted to the liberality of Lord
Panmure.

At the end of this Preface, are subjoined notes of a few char-


--- Aberbroth_page45.png ---


--- Aberbroth_page46.png ---


--- Aberbroth_page47.png ---
PREFACE. SNS

ters of more recent date, obligingly communicated by Mr, Robert-
son Chaplin,’ and the Earl of Northesk. These shew the ad-
ministration of the Abbacy wider successive and almost clashing
commendators, and give an approximation to the number of
monks within the Monastery, a very short time previous to the
Reformation.

The matter which follows consists of rentals and accounts
made up for the purpose of levying the revenues of the Abbey
for the general purposes of Church and State after the Reforma-
tion. They afford evidence of the extent of the Abbey posses-
sions, and of the manner of their occupation, almost from the
period when the independent existence of the Monastery may
be said to have ceased, to the time when its estates caine under
the usual administration of lay property.

The portrait of Cardinal David Betoun, which accompanies
this volume, was engraved at the expense of the late Mr. Chal-
mers of Aldbar, from a pieture believed to be an original. for-
erly in the Scots College at Rome, now in the Roman Catholic
College at Blairs.

The map of Angus and Mearns, begun by the kite Mr, Chal-
mers, and completed at the expense of his brother and heir, is
formed by a careful collation of numerous draughts and sketches

by Timothy Pont, Gordon of Straloch, and perhaps the parson of

--- Aberbroth_page48.png ---
XXX PREFACE.

tothiemay, prepared as the foundation of a map for the Scotch
volume of Blacu’s great Atlas. It is well known that the map of
Angus is wanting in that most valuable collection ; and the map
subsequently printed by Edwards, minister of Murroes, with a
corresponding sheet of letterpress, is very inferior to the work
superintended by Straloch and in great part executed by Pout
and him, Hardly any place or name is given in the present
map which is not to be found in their dranghts ; so that it pre-
sents the district as it was in the year 16-10, or a little earlier.

The seal engraved at the end of the Preface is that of Abbot
John Gedy, appended to the Act of Parliament for settling the
suecession to the Crown in 1371. The principal compartment
represents the murder of St. Thomas a-Becket, the patron of
Arbroath. The legend is :-—

SIGILL, ABBATIS SCI. THOME DE ABIRBROTIOC."

On the leaf opposite the title is the fine round chapter-seal of
Arbroath, which was used by the Convent for many ages. It
also represents the murder of Becket by the four conspirators ;
and persons of very clear sight, and knowing what to look for,
have found on two of the shields the arms of Fitazurse (a bear
rampant), and of Tracy (two bends).

The counter-seal of this stamp, not here engraved, represents
a shrine with the folding-doors open, displaying the Virgin and
child seated and crowned. The legend, which is not found legi-

Both etched on copper, with care and minute accuracy, by Mr, W. Smith.

the commone fele and our feil the gryntar beyng for the tyme - ande that

Ttem fen the rentale ma fufteyn the place that throw the negligens off
the granitar that the place fufteyn na falt bot that al the wyttallis be
brocht in as is befor writyn and that nayn be to craiff fra Pais till
Alhalowmes in the cuntre fen Gode off his grace hes giffyn the place
largely to liff apon and that fleuithnes off ofticiaris ger the place want
prouifione and that the abbot for the tyme beande that he be nocht
fleuithful na he tak cuir apone hym til ger all be fulfillit and inbrocht in
dew tyme as faid is in premiflis ande that the countis off the officiaris be

harde four tymys in the yeir.

In the quhilk court comperit the abbot with a part of the conuent of
Abirbrothoc and afkit deliuerans and determinacioun of the mater of Bal-
namonitmyre in fa far as wes ondeliuerit in the schireff court haldyn the
thrid day of O&ober inftant at the faid Myre eftir the tenour of oure foue-
vane Lordis lettres and att of court maid of befor and thar producit -
And than comperit Symone Banerman and allegit he haid and his mar-
rowis charteris and infeftmentis tharapone and for certane caufis mycht
nocht apprehend the famyn and afkit at the fehireff til fuperced quhil
the xxviii day of Nouember quhilk is Settyrday forow Andermes ewin
next to cum and than tha fuld cum and fchaw as tha wald wyn and tyne -

and confent of bath the parteis continewit the faid mater fa far as wes

370 REGISTRUM DE ABERBROTHOC.

[Inaentura de battella portus de Athy.]

This indenture maid at Abbirbroth the aucht day of the moneth of

September the yeir of Gode ane thoufande fywe hundreth and fex yeris

betuix ws George . . . abbot of . . . Abbirbroth . . . ande Thomas
lorde of Innermeith ande baron of Innerkelor . . . beiris vitnes . . .

as eftyr folowis . . . We George abbot forfaid with the confent of owr
conuent for hartly lufe we hawe to the faide lorde Thomas of owr fre wil
gefis licens to the faide Thomas lorde to bryng ane fifche boit in oure
hawyn of Athy for al the dayis of bis lywe + and his fifeharis to pas and
repas thairto with their fiuf and geir fik as ganis tham ande to lay thair
anchoris apon lande - to wis hyng ande dry nettis - to tak fifche - ande
all othir neceflar thingis to do vfe exers and hant neidful to fifching craft
ande fawyng of the boit and geir - quhilk vfe fal induce na poffeffione to
the faide lorde of Innermeith nothir grunde rycht nor feruitute bot fal be
alwais without preiudice or hurt til ws owr conuent ande place of Abbir-
broth and owre fucceffouris eftir the dede of the faide Thomas lorde - In
witnes heirof to a part of this indenture to ramane with ws . . . the
feille of Thomas lorde forfaid is appendit - ande to the part . . . to
ramane with the faid Thomas lorde we hayf appendit the commone {elle
of our chaptour .. . befor thir witnes - maftyr Hew Dowglace dene of

Brechin - Alexander Hepburn of Quhitfim - Adam Hepburn of the Crag

knyeht - Alexander Guthre of that ilk - James Haye of Nachtane

cht - Robert Gray fone to lord Gray - John Ogilwy of the Crage -
mattir Dauid Carale ande maftir James Farchar notairis - with othiris
diuers.

The abbot lets for nineteen years to Robert Lefly and Criftiane Wardlau
his wife, and to their heirs and affignees of no higher degree, the teind-
theaves of Innerpeffyr belonging to the faid Robert in heritage and
lying in the parifh of Arbroyth and diocete of Santtandros—paying
yearly 4 bolls of wheat, 10 bolls of bear, and 18 bolls of meal, to be
delivered at the granall of Arbroyth - 5 December 1527.

The abbot grants to Robert Lefly of Innerpeffyr for life a yearly penfion
of £10 Scots, on condition that he fall appear as procurator for the
abbot and convent in all caufes againtt all perfons, except thofe by whom
he has been previoully engaged, before the Lords of Council, Seffion,
and Parliament, and give them his counfel in the fame as often as

required - 5 December 1527.

540 CARTAE ORIGINALES.

Abirbrothoe - Omnia autem dona predicta ita liberaliter et quiete prefate
ecelefie concedo fieut ego terras meas proprias potiideo defenfione regni
mei excepta et regali iufticia fi abbas in curia fua aliqua negligencia de
jufticia deciderit - Quare rolo et firmiter precipio quod ipta ecclefia
Sancti Thome de Abirbrothoc et ipfius ccclefie abbas et conuentus
habeant et tencant in perpetuum omnia predita tenementa ct libertates
ficut fuperius determinatum eft cum terris ct cum omnibus pofleflionibus
ct cum omnibus tencmentis ecclefiatticis ct fecularibus que ego et fideles
mei contulimus et collaturi fumus bene et in pace libere et quicte integre
et plenarie et honorifice in botco ct plano in pratis ct pafturis in aquis et

molendinis in viuariis et ftagnis in pifeariis et marifeis in vattis et

fealingis in viis ct femitis et in omnibus aliis locis et aliis rebus ad eas
pertinentibus cum omnibus libertatibus et liberis confuetudinibus fuis
ficut eidem ecclefie concefli et hac carta mea confirmaui - Nee aliquis
fuceeflorum meorum aliquam aliam fecularem exaccionem ab eadem
ecclefia Sané&i Thome exigat - Teftibus Jocelino epitcopo Glafguentfi -
